summary_opm:
📌 Technical & Managerial Evaluation Comment for Jemmer Guilao (Project Superintendent – QAQC Engineering Team) - Evaluated by VKB


Jemmer Guilao obtained an average Technical & Managerial Evaluation score of 31% from November to February. Based on the company’s performance thresholds, this places him within the Project Superintendent 1 band (25.1%–33%), indicating that he is meeting the foundational expectations of the role while still developing the broader managerial and technical competencies required at higher superintendent levels.

📝 Context of the Evaluation

It is important to emphasize that the basis of this evaluation is his performance as a Project Engineer during the previous year, before he transitioned into the QAQC Engineering Team. As a Project Engineer, his responsibilities were primarily focused on:

  • site implementation,

  • coordination of daily activities,

  • execution of work plans, and

  • field supervision.

Because of this, he had limited exposure to higher‑level managerial functions such as strategic planning, documentation systems, quality assurance leadership, and resource management. His score of 31% accurately reflects this context and should be interpreted as a baseline, not a final measure of his capability in his current QAQC role.

📌 Upcoming QAQC‑Specific Evaluation Format

There is an ongoing development of a new evaluation format specifically designed for QAQC Engineers, ensuring that future assessments will be:

  • role‑based,

  • aligned with actual QAQC responsibilities, and

  • reflective of real performance outputs rather than generic project engineering metrics.

This new QAQC‑specific evaluation system will be introduced mid‑year 2026, allowing Jemmer and the rest of the QAQC Engineering Team to be assessed more accurately and fairly based on their true scope of work.

📊 Performance Summary

During the period evaluated, Jemmer demonstrated:

  • Basic capability in planning and executing site activities

  • Developing understanding of quality assurance and documentation

  • Willingness to comply with reporting and coordination requirements

  • Positive attitude toward learning and adapting to new systems

  • Growing awareness of safety, schedule adherence, and resource coordination

However, the evaluation also highlights:

  • Limited managerial depth due to his previous role’s scope

  • Gaps in documentation accuracy and timeliness

  • Opportunities to improve analytical decision‑making and technical judgment

  • Limited exposure to innovation, cost optimization, and system improvement

🌱 Overall Assessment

Jemmer’s current score of 31% reflects his performance during a period when he was still functioning as a Project Engineer. As he transitions fully into the QAQC Engineering Team, his responsibilities, exposure, and technical depth will naturally expand. With the upcoming QAQC‑specific evaluation format launching mid‑2026, his future assessments will more accurately capture his capabilities and contributions.
summary_qaqc:
📌 QAQC Evaluation Comment for Jemmer Guilao (Project Superintendent / QAQC Engineer)


Jemmer Guilao’s QAQC evaluation score places him within the Project Superintendent 2 performance band (33.1%–42%), reflecting a solid developing competency in quality governance, documentation oversight, and field decision‑making. His score demonstrates that he is performing above the minimum expectations for a superintendent and is progressing toward higher‑level QAQC leadership responsibilities.

📝 Context of the Evaluation

As this QAQC evaluation system is newly implemented, the entire team—including Jemmer—is still adjusting to the structured scoring method, expanded criteria, and stricter documentation requirements. Naturally, this first-cycle implementation has resulted in generally lower scores across all positions. Jemmer’s rating should therefore be interpreted as a baseline for growth, not a final measure of capability.

Given his dual role as Project Superintendent and QAQC Engineer, Jemmer carries a higher expectation for technical mastery, documentation accuracy, and process discipline. His current score reflects both his strengths and the areas where deeper QAQC expertise is still needed.

📌 Key Expectation for a QAQC Engineer

As a QAQC Engineer, Jemmer is expected to master the entire QAQC process, not only in theory but in practical, real‑world application. This includes:

  • Understanding how each QAQC step affects cost, schedule, and compliance

  • Anticipating quality risks before they escalate into non‑conformances

  • Improving the QAQC system based on realistic site situations and constraints

  • Ensuring that inspection, testing, and documentation processes are consistently followed

Mastery of these areas is essential for him to progress toward higher superintendent or managerial tiers.

📈 Recommendations for Improvement

To advance toward the next performance tier (Project Superintendent 3: 42.1%–50%), Jemmer is encouraged to focus on the following:

1. Deepen Mastery of QAQC Processes

  • Strengthen understanding of ITPs, method statements, and hold points

  • Improve coordination of testing schedules and inspection readiness

  • Ensure timely and accurate submission of IRs, test results, and NCR documentation

2. Enhance System Improvement Initiatives

  • Propose practical adjustments to QAQC workflows based on actual site challenges

  • Identify recurring issues and recommend preventive measures

  • Support digital archiving and ISO‑aligned documentation practices

3. Strengthen Documentation Accuracy and Traceability

  • Maintain audit‑ready records for NCRs, CAPAs, RFIs, and test logs

  • Ensure all documentation is complete, consistent, and properly filed

  • Improve the speed and accuracy of closing punch‑list items and NCs

4. Improve Proactive Quality Leadership

  • Conduct internal pre‑inspection checks to prevent rejections

  • Mentor site personnel on quality discipline and inspection readiness

  • Communicate quality risks early to avoid delays and rework
summary_qs:
📌 QS Evaluation Comment for Jemmer Guilao (Project Superintendent – QAQC Engineering Team)


Jemmer Guilao obtained an average QS evaluation score of 22% from November to February. Based on the company’s performance thresholds, this places him below the Project Superintendent 1 band (25.1%–33%), indicating that his current QS competency is still at an early developmental stage. This score is consistent with his background and the nature of his previous responsibilities.

📝 Context of the Evaluation

It is important to emphasize that this QS evaluation system is new, and the entire team—including Jemmer—is still adjusting to the structured scoring method, expanded criteria, and stricter documentation requirements. Naturally, this first-cycle implementation has resulted in lower scores across all positions. Jemmer’s rating should therefore be viewed as a baseline for growth, not a final measure of capability.

Additionally, Jemmer’s earlier role as a Project Engineer was heavily focused on site implementation, where the primary responsibilities involve execution, coordination, and field supervision. Because of this, his exposure to deeper Quantity Surveying functions—such as detailed cost analysis, value engineering, and financial tracking—was limited. This explains why his QS score is low and why certain analytical and cost‑related competencies are still developing.

Despite this, Jemmer is now part of the QAQC Engineering and Design Team, which exposes him to all disciplines of construction. This multidisciplinary environment will naturally strengthen his understanding of cost implications, design coordination, and QS‑related processes as he becomes more involved in planning and technical decision‑making.

📈 Recommendations for Improvement

To progress toward the Project Superintendent 1 band (25.1%–33%) and eventually higher tiers, Jemmer is encouraged to focus on the following:

1. Strengthen Measurement & Cost Estimation Skills

  • Review actual site outputs and productivity rates to validate estimates

  • Improve analytical precision in quantity take‑offs

  • Apply data‑driven methods when preparing estimates

  • Compare estimated costs with actual expenditures to identify gaps

2. Enhance Cost Control & Value Management Awareness

  • Track expenditures more consistently against approved budgets

  • Understand the financial impact of design changes and site decisions

  • Prepare variation order requests with complete and justified documentation

  • Recommend cost‑efficient alternatives where applicable

3. Leverage His Role in the Design Team

  • Engage actively with architects, engineers, and planners to understand cost drivers

  • Use design coordination meetings to refine cost assumptions

  • Learn how design decisions translate into material quantities and labor requirements

🌱 Overall Assessment

Jemmer is in a strong position to grow within the QAQC Engineering Team. His current score of 22% reflects both the learning curve of the new QS evaluation system and the natural limitations of his previous site‑focused role. As he becomes more engaged with QS processes through his involvement in the design team, he is expected to significantly improve his analytical skills, cost awareness, and overall QS performance in the coming evaluation cycles.
